title: Container With Most Water
code: left++;    // shorter wall on the left -> move it
right--;   // shorter (or equal) wall on the right -> move it

frames.caption: Initialize: left = 0, right = 8, best = 0. The widest possible container spans the full array.
frames.note: Converging two pointers starts at the two ends to get the maximum width for free. Unlike sorted Two Sum, the input here is NOT sorted — converging still works because the invariant is about width, not value order: width = R − L can only shrink as the pointers move inward, so the search is bounded by n. We carry just three ints (left, right, best), giving O(1) extra space.

frames.caption: area = (8 − 0) × min(1, 7) = 8 × 1 = 8.
frames.note: The water a pair can hold is width × the SHORTER of the two walls — overflow spills over the lower rim, so the taller wall is wasted height. That is why area uses Math.Min, not the sum or the max. Computing this is O(1); doing it once per pointer move (never for all pairs) is the whole speedup over the O(n²) brute force.

frames.caption: 8 > previous best → new best = 8.
frames.note: best is the running maximum: the largest area any container examined so far could hold. We never need to revisit earlier pairs because the greedy move rule guarantees the ones we skipped could not have beaten this. Keeping a single scalar best is all the bookkeeping an O(1)-space solution needs.

frames.caption: height[left] = 1 < height[right] = 7: left wall is shorter → left++ = 1.
frames.note: Width (R − L) shrinks on every move, so the only way to ever beat the current area is to raise the limiting height — and the limit is min(h[L], h[R]). Moving the TALLER wall can never help: the new pair is still capped by the shorter wall you kept (or lower) while the width drops. So you always advance the shorter wall. That single move safely discards every container that used it — none could be wider, and their height was already capped by that wall. This greedy rule is exactly what keeps the whole sweep O(n) instead of the brute-force O(n²) over all pairs.

frames.caption: area = (8 − 1) × min(8, 7) = 7 × 7 = 49.
frames.note: The water a pair can hold is width × the SHORTER of the two walls — overflow spills over the lower rim, so the taller wall is wasted height. That is why area uses Math.Min, not the sum or the max. Computing this is O(1); doing it once per pointer move (never for all pairs) is the whole speedup over the O(n²) brute force.

frames.caption: 49 > previous best → new best = 49.
frames.note: best is the running maximum: the largest area any container examined so far could hold. We never need to revisit earlier pairs because the greedy move rule guarantees the ones we skipped could not have beaten this. Keeping a single scalar best is all the bookkeeping an O(1)-space solution needs.

frames.caption: height[right] = 7 ≤ height[left] = 8: right wall is shorter → right-- = 7.
frames.note: Width (R − L) shrinks on every move, so the only way to ever beat the current area is to raise the limiting height — and the limit is min(h[L], h[R]). Moving the TALLER wall can never help: the new pair is still capped by the shorter wall you kept (or lower) while the width drops. So you always advance the shorter wall. That single move safely discards every container that used it — none could be wider, and their height was already capped by that wall. This greedy rule is exactly what keeps the whole sweep O(n) instead of the brute-force O(n²) over all pairs.

frames.caption: area = (7 − 1) × min(8, 3) = 6 × 3 = 18.
frames.note: The water a pair can hold is width × the SHORTER of the two walls — overflow spills over the lower rim, so the taller wall is wasted height. That is why area uses Math.Min, not the sum or the max. Computing this is O(1); doing it once per pointer move (never for all pairs) is the whole speedup over the O(n²) brute force.

frames.caption: 18 ≤ best (49) → best stays 49.
frames.note: A smaller area does not update best — but the step is not wasted: it still discards a container so the pointers keep converging. Because width is now strictly smaller than when best was set, only a taller limiting wall could ever catch up, which is precisely what advancing the shorter wall hunts for.

frames.caption: height[right] = 3 ≤ height[left] = 8: right wall is shorter → right-- = 6.
frames.note: Width (R − L) shrinks on every move, so the only way to ever beat the current area is to raise the limiting height — and the limit is min(h[L], h[R]). Moving the TALLER wall can never help: the new pair is still capped by the shorter wall you kept (or lower) while the width drops. So you always advance the shorter wall. That single move safely discards every container that used it — none could be wider, and their height was already capped by that wall. This greedy rule is exactly what keeps the whole sweep O(n) instead of the brute-force O(n²) over all pairs.

frames.caption: area = (6 − 1) × min(8, 8) = 5 × 8 = 40.
frames.note: The water a pair can hold is width × the SHORTER of the two walls — overflow spills over the lower rim, so the taller wall is wasted height. That is why area uses Math.Min, not the sum or the max. Computing this is O(1); doing it once per pointer move (never for all pairs) is the whole speedup over the O(n²) brute force.

frames.caption: 40 ≤ best (49) → best stays 49.
frames.note: A smaller area does not update best — but the step is not wasted: it still discards a container so the pointers keep converging. Because width is now strictly smaller than when best was set, only a taller limiting wall could ever catch up, which is precisely what advancing the shorter wall hunts for.

frames.caption: height[left] = 8 == height[right] = 8: tie → move right by convention, right-- = 5.
frames.note: When the two walls are equal, either one can move — moving either discards a wall that is already the limiting height, and no untried container keeping it could be both wider and taller. By convention this code takes the else branch and moves R. The area cannot improve from this exact pair regardless of choice, so the tie is a free move inward.

frames.caption: area = (5 − 1) × min(8, 4) = 4 × 4 = 16.
frames.note: The water a pair can hold is width × the SHORTER of the two walls — overflow spills over the lower rim, so the taller wall is wasted height. That is why area uses Math.Min, not the sum or the max. Computing this is O(1); doing it once per pointer move (never for all pairs) is the whole speedup over the O(n²) brute force.

frames.caption: 16 ≤ best (49) → best stays 49.
frames.note: A smaller area does not update best — but the step is not wasted: it still discards a container so the pointers keep converging. Because width is now strictly smaller than when best was set, only a taller limiting wall could ever catch up, which is precisely what advancing the shorter wall hunts for.

frames.caption: height[right] = 4 ≤ height[left] = 8: right wall is shorter → right-- = 4.
frames.note: Width (R − L) shrinks on every move, so the only way to ever beat the current area is to raise the limiting height — and the limit is min(h[L], h[R]). Moving the TALLER wall can never help: the new pair is still capped by the shorter wall you kept (or lower) while the width drops. So you always advance the shorter wall. That single move safely discards every container that used it — none could be wider, and their height was already capped by that wall. This greedy rule is exactly what keeps the whole sweep O(n) instead of the brute-force O(n²) over all pairs.

frames.caption: area = (4 − 1) × min(8, 5) = 3 × 5 = 15.
frames.note: The water a pair can hold is width × the SHORTER of the two walls — overflow spills over the lower rim, so the taller wall is wasted height. That is why area uses Math.Min, not the sum or the max. Computing this is O(1); doing it once per pointer move (never for all pairs) is the whole speedup over the O(n²) brute force.

frames.caption: height[right] = 5 ≤ height[left] = 8: right wall is shorter → right-- = 3.
frames.note: Width (R − L) shrinks on every move, so the only way to ever beat the current area is to raise the limiting height — and the limit is min(h[L], h[R]). That is why area uses Math.Min, not the sum or the max. Computing this is O(1); doing it once per pointer move (never for all pairs) is the whole speedup over the O(n²) brute force.

frames.note: A smaller area does not update best — but the step is not wasted: it still discards a container so the pointers keep converging. Because width is now strictly smaller than when best was set, only a taller limiting wall could ever catch up, which is precisely what advancing the shorter wall hunts for.

frames.caption: height[right] = 2 ≤ height[left] = 8: right wall is shorter → right-- = 2.
frames.note: Width (R − L) shrinks on every move, so the only way to ever beat the current area is to raise the limiting height — and the limit is min(h[L], h[R]). Moving the TALLER wall can never help: the new pair is still capped by the shorter wall you kept (or lower) while the width drops. So you always advance the shorter wall. frames.caption: area = (2 − 1) × min(8, 6) = 1 × 6 = 6.
frames.note: The water a pair can hold is width × the SHORTER of the two walls — overflow spills over the lower rim, so the taller wall is wasted height. That is why area uses Math.Min, not the sum or the max. Computing this is O(1); doing it once per pointer move (never for all pairs) is the whole speedup over the O(n²) brute force.

frames.note: A smaller area does not update best — but the step is not wasted: it still discards a container so the pointers keep converging. frames.caption: height[right] = 6 ≤ height[left] = 8: right wall is shorter → right-- = 1.
frames.note: Width (R − L) shrinks on every move, so the only way to ever beat the current area is to raise the limiting height — and the limit is min(h[L], h[R]). Moving the TALLER wall can never help: the new pair is still capped by the shorter wall you kept (or lower) while the width drops. So you always advance the shorter wall. frames.caption: left (1) meets right (1): the window is empty, loop ends. Return best = 49.
frames.note: When left meets right the width is 0 — no container is left to try, and every move only shrank the width, so nothing wider remains unexplored. The two pointers together made at most n − 1 moves, so the whole sweep is O(n) time and O(1) space, versus the O(n²) brute force that scores every one of the ~n²/2 pairs.
